title: TBM 330: (Un)common Sense Playbook
questions: How can an engineering manager stabilize a struggling team without losing momentum for experimentation?
What enables a commitment pause or “time out” to succeed with stakeholders and the team?
Why do otherwise competent managers struggle to get buy-in on basic changes?
When should the problem be framed as environment and conditions rather than individual manager skill?
search_terms: how to stabilize a team temporary pause
time out social capital stakeholder management
trust and psychological safety after layoffs
summary: The post outlines a “common sense” engineering management playbook for turning around struggling teams: align on the why, engage customers, stabilize via a temporary pause, set metrics and cadence, and gradually reintroduce work. It’s aimed at engineering leaders and managers who want to understand why these tactics sometimes fail despite being straightforward. The author argues that execution depends heavily on environmental conditions—optics pressure, toxic influence, trust, and partner-team capacity—not just manager skill.

title: TBM 235: Forms & Shadows
questions: Why do some product teams feel coherent while others constantly shift priorities and translate between contexts?
How does organizational structure interfere with implementing value-and-customer-first ways of working?
What causes decision drift and “why are we doing this again?” energy in product teams?
What interventions can reduce cognitive load without locking the organization into rigid processes?
search_terms: cognitive load from context switching teams
summary: The post argues that product teams either thrive or struggle depending on whether key organizational “frames” (strategy, structure, customers, incentives, goals, releases, knowledge, etc.) are coherent with each other. It’s aimed at product leaders and practitioners who want to diagnose symptoms like context switching, decision drift, and misaligned priorities, and who are considering interventions such as temporary scaffolding and attractors to restore alignment.

title: TBM 218: Level, Depth, Time, and Frame
questions: How do I balance being high-level with being specific without losing strategic coherence?
How often should I update strategy templates and roadmaps across different time horizons?
What strategy “frame” should I use when communicating with different stakeholders?
How do I align quarterly plans, annual goals, and multi-year strategy so they reinforce each other?
search_terms: strategy dimensions level specificity time frame
Rumelt strategy diagnosis guiding principles coherent actions
summary: The post proposes a practical way to think about strategy by mapping it to four dimensions: level, depth (specificity), time horizon, and frame. It’s aimed at product managers and strategists who feel pulled between vague big-picture thinking and demands for concrete details, and it helps them choose the right strategy expression and update cadence. It also offers a mental model for how strategy artifacts and refresh cycles can coexist across multiple time horizons.

title: TBM 215: Shallow vs. Deep Alignment
questions: How do we distinguish shallow alignment from deep alignment in our team?
What does it mean to treat alignment as a continuous system rather than a checkbox?
Why do OKRs and action items fail to guarantee true alignment?
How can we create psychological safety to support disagreement and tension during alignment?
search_terms: how to maintain alignment over time
summary: This post argues that organizational alignment is a continuous, adaptive process of building shared purpose and understanding, not a one-time achievement or slide-ready consensus. It contrasts shallow alignment (low-tension, artifact-driven) with deep alignment (messy, tension-including, requiring psychological safety), offering guidance for teams that rely too heavily on OKRs and other proxies. It is aimed at leaders and managers who want more resilient, creative alignment practices.

title: TBM 46/52: (F)acts, (A)ssumptions, (B)eliefs
questions: How can our team quickly get aligned on the foundational premises behind our work?
Which of our assumptions are we operating on now versus testing for the future?
How do we track and update facts, assumptions, and beliefs over time without losing context?
What should we do when everything seems to change all the time?
summary: The post proposes a lightweight team exercise—facts, assumptions, beliefs (FAB)—to improve alignment on the underlying premises driving day-to-day work. It’s aimed at teams and leaders who want a practical way to distinguish operating assumptions from hypotheses to test, assign confidence levels, and create a recurring review ritual with historical context. It also extends the idea to both company-wide and team-level alignment (fractal FAB).

title: TBM 43/52: Clarity & Inspiration
questions: Does the need to inspire engineers bias product managers toward certainty theater?
What tactics help inspire engineering when uncertainty is high?
How much inspiration is enough, and can teams be too inspired?
If engineering can push back but doesn't, what does that indicate about strategy clarity and organizational dynamics?
search_terms: single owner clarity vs shared ownership
summary: The post explores how product strategy clarity is shaped by the need to inspire engineering, especially under uncertainty. It uses the drivers/constraints/floats framing as a backdrop and raises questions about whether inspiration can lead to certainty theater, when clarity is demanded by forcing functions, and what engineering pushback reveals about organizational design. It is aimed at product leaders and teams thinking about how to align strategy and execution.

title: TBM 42/52: Fluffy & Ambiguous
questions: How do I write objectives that leave room for creativity without becoming fluffy and ambiguous?
Why do my PMs seem to agree but produce roadmaps that feel scattered?
What should I do when newer PMs lack the implicit context that made earlier alignment easy?
How can I tell whether an objective is specific enough to inspire coherent actions and good questions?
search_terms: onboarding PMs with context and clarity
summary: This post helps product leaders and PM orgs craft objectives and guiding principles that preserve creativity while avoiding ambiguity that causes misaligned roadmaps. It explains why newer PMs may respond with ambivalence when they lack the implicit context that more tenured PMs rely on, and offers practical tests for whether objectives are specific, opinionated, and question-inviting. Intended for product leaders, product managers, and teams using OKR-style planning who want clearer strategy without over-prescription.

title: TBM 38/52: Navigating the Product Leadership Fog
questions: How do I handle the gap between corporate strategy and day-to-day plans when priorities keep changing?
What should I do when both strategic thinkers and get-it-done thinkers are frustrated and misaligned?
How do I rebuild trust and psychological safety when the team is in blame mode and people are leaving?
What does “good enough” strategy look like when we don’t have time to think strategically?
search_terms: degrading trust psychological safety product team
how to relieve teams from filling the void
summary: This post offers guidance for product leaders navigating organizational confusion during rapid growth and constant priority shifts. It frames the core issue as a “leadership fog” driven by degrading trust and psychological safety, and it distinguishes the different needs of strategic thinkers versus get-it-done thinkers. It’s aimed at leaders who feel pressure to produce perfect strategy and plan but need practical steps to restore trust, reduce blame, and create cadence with a good-enough strategy.

title: TBM 27b/52: Why Most Strategies Lack Clarity
questions: Why do so many product strategies feel vague or incoherent?
Is it possible to have a clear strategy without being certain?
How should leaders communicate uncertainty to stakeholders who demand certainty?
What causes leaders to hedge or obfuscate strategy messaging?
search_terms: Miro mandate levels competitive analysis can should templates
summary: The post argues that many product strategies lack clarity because leaders equate coherence with certainty and then avoid uncertainty due to fear and stakeholder pressure. It’s aimed at product and strategy leaders who need a way to communicate a coherent direction while explicitly acknowledging what is known, assumed, and believed.

title: TBM 18/52: Product Manager to People Manager (and My Big Mistake)
questions: What does clarity look and feel like for each person on my team?
How do I align on strategy and plans when different people define them differently?
What should I do when cross-functional clarity needs conflict?
How can a former product manager unlearn informal influence and build effective people management habits?
search_terms: product manager to people manager transition
how to ask what clarity looks like
unlearning product management habits for leadership
summary: The post is a practical reflection for experienced individual contributors—especially product managers—who are transitioning into people management after a long hiatus. It argues that alignment fails when leaders assume shared definitions of “clarity” and instead recommends repeatedly asking individuals what clarity looks and feels like, then digging into the underlying human needs. It also highlights how cross-functional teams can have opposing clarity requirements and why formal authority and decisive action become essential.

title: TBM 16/52: Goal Clarity (and Embracing Different Perspectives)
questions: Why do teams stay misaligned even when everyone agrees on the same goal statement?
How can we make goals clearer without over-specifying solutions?
What conversations should we have to surface assumptions and out-of-scope boundaries in a goal?
How do we handle different experience levels when interpreting and acting on goals?
search_terms: interpretation gaps in OKRs or quarterly goals
why goals fail to align teams
summary: The post argues that even a well-written quarterly goal will be interpreted differently by team members, leading to misalignment. It is aimed at managers and team leads who want practical ways to improve goal clarity through shared understanding, explicit conversations about interpretations and decision impact, and psychological safety to iterate on goals. It also emphasizes respecting different experience levels so that newer or less confident contributors can engage meaningfully.

title: TBM 51/53: Coherence > Shallow Autonomy
questions: How do you empower teams without falling into shallow autonomy?
What does it mean to frame a coherent strategy that enables creativity and agency?
How much should leadership be opinionated, and at what level?
What kind of context do passionate team members actually need to stay aligned?
summary: The post argues that effective empowerment requires leaders to provide a coherent, evidence-backed, and appropriately opinionated strategy—not just vague vision statements or overly prescriptive planning. It is aimed at leaders and product/strategy practitioners who want to enable creativity and agency while keeping teams aligned and avoiding mediocrity. It also challenges the assumption that autonomy always means bottom-up planning.

title: TBM 24/53: Mapping Beliefs (and Agreeing to Disagree)
questions: Which team beliefs are worth converging on, and which convergence is actually harmful?
When should we agree, and when should we agree to disagree?
How do we surface and map beliefs when people struggle to articulate them?
How can we tell when agreement has become stale and needs to be re-examined?
summary: The post introduces a simple belief-convergence matrix for teams to place shared beliefs based on how much they converge and whether that convergence helps or harms outcomes. It’s aimed at leaders and facilitators who want better conversations about product strategy, executive disagreements, and when to shift from alignment to agreeing to disagree. It also highlights how agreement can become stale and how re-challenging it can create productive uncertainty before moving back toward convergence.
